instruction: Implement profile-guided optimization (PGO) workflow in C++ and explain how it improves performance. Show how to instrument code, collect profiles, and use them for optimization. Include a benchmark demonstrating the improvement.
output:
<think>
Profile-Guided Optimization (PGO) is a compiler optimization technique that uses runtime profiling data to make better optimization decisions. Instead of the compiler guessing which code paths are hot (based on heuristics), PGO tells it exactly which paths are hot based on real execution.

PGO workflow:
1. Build with instrumentation (-fprofile-generate / -fprofile-instr-generate)
2. Run the instrumented binary with representative workloads
3. Merge profile data
4. Rebuild with profile data (-fprofile-use / -fprofile-instr-use)

Optimizations PGO enables:
- Better branch prediction hints: hot branches get fall-through layout
- Function inlining: inline only functions that are actually called frequently
- Code layout: place hot code together for better instruction cache utilization
- Register allocation: prioritize hot paths
- Vectorization: loop trip counts inform vectorization decisions
- Indirect call promotion: devirtualize based on observed call targets

Typical speedups: 10-30% for compute-heavy workloads. More for branch-heavy code (parsers, interpreters, databases).

I'll implement a PGO-friendly benchmark that demonstrates the improvement, along with the build scripts for both GCC and Clang.
</think>

```cpp
// pgo_benchmark.cpp - Demonstrates PGO optimization
// 
// Build script (save as build_pgo.sh):
// #!/bin/bash
// # Step 1: Build with instrumentation
// clang++ -O2 -fprofile-instr-generate -o bench_instrumented pgo_benchmark.cpp
// 
// # Step 2: Run with representative workload to collect profile
// ./bench_instrumented
// 
// # Step 3: Merge profile data
// llvm-profdata merge -o default.profdata default.profraw
// 
// # Step 4: Build with profile data
// clang++ -O2 -fprofile-instr-use=default.profdata -o bench_pgo pgo_benchmark.cpp
// 
// # Step 5: Build without PGO for comparison
// clang++ -O2 -o bench_nopgo pgo_benchmark.cpp
//
// # GCC equivalent:
// # g++ -O2 -fprofile-generate -o bench_instrumented pgo_benchmark.cpp
// # ./bench_instrumented
// # g++ -O2 -fprofile-use -o bench_pgo pgo_benchmark.cpp

#include <algorithm>
#include <chrono>
#include <cstdint>
#include <iostream>
#include <numeric>
#include <random>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

// === Branch-heavy code: benefits most from PGO ===

// Simulates a packet classifier with many branches
enum class PacketType : uint8_t {
    TCP = 0, UDP = 1, ICMP = 2, DNS = 3,
    HTTP = 4, HTTPS = 5, SSH = 6, OTHER = 7
};

struct Packet {
    PacketType type;
    uint16_t src_port;
    uint16_t dst_port;
    uint32_t payload_size;
    uint8_t flags;
};

// Without PGO, compiler doesn't know HTTP is 70% of traffic
// With PGO, compiler optimizes the HTTP branch to be the fast path
uint64_t classify_packet(const Packet& pkt) {
    switch (pkt.type) {
        case PacketType::TCP:
            if (pkt.flags & 0x02) return 1;  // SYN
            if (pkt.flags & 0x01) return 2;  // FIN
            return 3;
        case PacketType::UDP:
            if (pkt.dst_port == 53) return 10;  // DNS
            if (pkt.dst_port == 5060) return 11; // SIP
            return 12;
        case PacketType::HTTP:
            // PGO learns this is the hot path (70% of packets)
            if (pkt.payload_size > 1000) return 20; // Large response
            if (pkt.payload_size > 100) return 21;  // Medium
            return 22;  // Small
        case PacketType::HTTPS:
            return 30;
        case PacketType::DNS:
            return 40;
        case PacketType::SSH:
            return 50;
        case PacketType::ICMP:
            return 60;
        default:
            return 99;
    }
}

// === Indirect call code: PGO enables devirtualization ===

struct Processor {
    virtual uint64_t process(uint64_t value) const = 0;
    virtual ~Processor() = default;
};

struct FastProcessor : Processor {
    uint64_t process(uint64_t value) const override {
        return value * 2 + 1;
    }
};

struct SlowProcessor : Processor {
    uint64_t process(uint64_t value) const override {
        uint64_t result = value;
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) {
            result = (result ^ (result << 13)) * 0x5DEECE66D;
        }
        return result;
    }
};

// PGO learns FastProcessor is called 90% of the time
// and can speculatively devirtualize the call
uint64_t process_batch(
    const std::vector<const Processor*>& processors,
    const std::vector<uint64_t>& values
) {
    uint64_t sum = 0;
    for (size_t i = 0; i < values.size(); ++i) {
        sum += processors[i % processors.size()]->process(values[i]);
    }
    return sum;
}

// === Benchmark ===

template <typename Func>
double benchmark_ms(Func&& f, int iterations = 5) {
    double best = 1e18;
    for (int i = 0; i < iterations; ++i) {
        auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        f();
        auto end = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        double ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(end - start).count();
        if (ms < best) best = ms;
    }
    return best;
}

int main() {
    std::mt19937 rng(42);
    constexpr size_t N = 10'000'000;

    // Generate packets with realistic distribution
    // HTTP: 70%, HTTPS: 15%, TCP: 5%, UDP: 5%, other: 5%
    std::vector<Packet> packets(N);
    std::discrete_distribution<int> type_dist({5, 5, 1, 1, 70, 15, 2, 1});

    for (auto& pkt : packets) {
        pkt.type = static_cast<PacketType>(type_dist(rng));
        pkt.src_port = rng() % 65536;
        pkt.dst_port = rng() % 65536;
        pkt.payload_size = rng() % 10000;
        pkt.flags = rng() % 256;
    }

    // Benchmark packet classification
    uint64_t result = 0;
    double t_classify = benchmark_ms([&]() {
        result = 0;
        for (const auto& pkt : packets) {
            result += classify_packet(pkt);
        }
    });

    std::cout << "Packet classification (" << N << " packets): "
              << t_classify << " ms (sum=" << result << ")" << std::endl;

    // Benchmark virtual dispatch
    FastProcessor fast;
    SlowProcessor slow;
    // 90% fast, 10% slow — PGO should devirtualize the fast path
    std::vector<const Processor*> procs;
    for (int i = 0; i < 10; ++i) {
        procs.push_back(i < 9 ? static_cast<const Processor*>(&fast) : &slow);
    }

    std::vector<uint64_t> values(N);
    std::iota(values.begin(), values.end(), 0);

    uint64_t vresult = 0;
    double t_virtual = benchmark_ms([&]() {
        vresult = process_batch(procs, values);
    });

    std::cout << "Virtual dispatch (" << N << " calls): "
              << t_virtual << " ms (sum=" << vresult << ")" << std::endl;

    std::cout << "\nRun with PGO to see ~10-30% improvement on branch-heavy code."
              << std::endl;

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement SIMD intrinsics in C++ for common data processing operations: sum array, find maximum, string search, and dot product. Show both SSE and AVX versions with proper fallback for unsupported hardware.
output:
<think>
SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) processes multiple data elements in parallel using wide registers. SSE uses 128-bit registers (4 floats or 2 doubles), AVX uses 256-bit (8 floats or 4 doubles), AVX-512 uses 512-bit.

Key operations to implement:
1. Array sum: add groups of 4/8 floats at once, horizontal sum at the end
2. Find maximum: compare groups, track running max vector, horizontal max at end
3. String search (memchr-like): compare 16/32 bytes at once for a target byte
4. Dot product: multiply and accumulate in parallel

For each, I need:
- Scalar fallback (always works)
- SSE version (most x86-64 CPUs since 2003)
- AVX2 version (Intel Haswell+, AMD Excavator+)
- Runtime dispatch: detect CPU features and choose the best version

Runtime dispatch is important: compile all versions, check CPUID at startup, use function pointers. This avoids requiring a minimum CPU level.

Alignment matters: SIMD loads are faster when aligned to 16/32 bytes. Misaligned loads work on modern CPUs but may be slower. I'll handle the alignment edge cases.

I'll use the intrinsics headers: <immintrin.h> (includes everything).
</think>

```cpp
#include <immintrin.h>
#include <chrono>
#include <cmath>
#include <cstring>
#include <iostream>
#include <numeric>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

// === CPU Feature Detection ===

struct CPUFeatures {
    bool sse2 = false;
    bool sse4_1 = false;
    bool avx = false;
    bool avx2 = false;

    static CPUFeatures detect() {
        CPUFeatures f;
        #ifdef __x86_64__
        unsigned int eax, ebx, ecx, edx;
        __cpuid(1, eax, ebx, ecx, edx);
        f.sse2 = (edx >> 26) & 1;
        f.sse4_1 = (ecx >> 19) & 1;
        f.avx = (ecx >> 28) & 1;
        __cpuid_count(7, 0, eax, ebx, ecx, edx);
        f.avx2 = (ebx >> 5) & 1;
        #endif
        return f;
    }
};

// === 1. Array Sum ===

float sum_scalar(const float* data, size_t n) {
    float sum = 0;
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) sum += data[i];
    return sum;
}

#ifdef __SSE2__
float sum_sse(const float* data, size_t n) {
    __m128 vsum = _mm_setzero_ps();
    size_t i = 0;

    // Process 4 floats at a time
    for (; i + 4 <= n; i += 4) {
        __m128 v = _mm_loadu_ps(data + i);
        vsum = _mm_add_ps(vsum, v);
    }

    // Horizontal sum of 4 lanes
    __m128 shuf = _mm_movehdup_ps(vsum);
    __m128 sums = _mm_add_ps(vsum, shuf);
    shuf = _mm_movehl_ps(shuf, sums);
    sums = _mm_add_ss(sums, shuf);
    float result = _mm_cvtss_f32(sums);

    // Handle remaining elements
    for (; i < n; ++i) result += data[i];
    return result;
}
#endif

#ifdef __AVX__
float sum_avx(const float* data, size_t n) {
    __m256 vsum = _mm256_setzero_ps();
    size_t i = 0;

    // Process 8 floats at a time
    for (; i + 8 <= n; i += 8) {
        __m256 v = _mm256_loadu_ps(data + i);
        vsum = _mm256_add_ps(vsum, v);
    }

    // Horizontal sum: 256 -> 128 -> scalar
    __m128 hi = _mm256_extractf128_ps(vsum, 1);
    __m128 lo = _mm256_castps256_ps128(vsum);
    __m128 sum128 = _mm_add_ps(lo, hi);
    __m128 shuf = _mm_movehdup_ps(sum128);
    __m128 sums = _mm_add_ps(sum128, shuf);
    shuf = _mm_movehl_ps(shuf, sums);
    sums = _mm_add_ss(sums, shuf);
    float result = _mm_cvtss_f32(sums);

    for (; i < n; ++i) result += data[i];
    return result;
}
#endif

// === 2. Dot Product ===

float dot_scalar(const float* a, const float* b, size_t n) {
    float sum = 0;
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) sum += a[i] * b[i];
    return sum;
}

#ifdef __AVX__
float dot_avx(const float* a, const float* b, size_t n) {
    __m256 vsum = _mm256_setzero_ps();
    size_t i = 0;

    for (; i + 8 <= n; i += 8) {
        __m256 va = _mm256_loadu_ps(a + i);
        __m256 vb = _mm256_loadu_ps(b + i);
        vsum = _mm256_fmadd_ps(va, vb, vsum);  // FMA: a*b + sum
    }

    // Horizontal sum
    __m128 hi = _mm256_extractf128_ps(vsum, 1);
    __m128 lo = _mm256_castps256_ps128(vsum);
    __m128 sum128 = _mm_add_ps(lo, hi);
    sum128 = _mm_hadd_ps(sum128, sum128);
    sum128 = _mm_hadd_ps(sum128, sum128);
    float result = _mm_cvtss_f32(sum128);

    for (; i < n; ++i) result += a[i] * b[i];
    return result;
}
#endif

// === 3. Find Maximum ===

float max_scalar(const float* data, size_t n) {
    float m = data[0];
    for (size_t i = 1; i < n; ++i) {
        if (data[i] > m) m = data[i];
    }
    return m;
}

#ifdef __AVX__
float max_avx(const float* data, size_t n) {
    __m256 vmax = _mm256_set1_ps(-INFINITY);
    size_t i = 0;

    for (; i + 8 <= n; i += 8) {
        __m256 v = _mm256_loadu_ps(data + i);
        vmax = _mm256_max_ps(vmax, v);
    }

    // Horizontal max
    __m128 hi = _mm256_extractf128_ps(vmax, 1);
    __m128 lo = _mm256_castps256_ps128(vmax);
    __m128 max128 = _mm_max_ps(lo, hi);
    max128 = _mm_max_ps(max128, _mm_movehl_ps(max128, max128));
    max128 = _mm_max_ss(max128, _mm_movehdup_ps(max128));
    float result = _mm_cvtss_f32(max128);

    for (; i < n; ++i) {
        if (data[i] > result) result = data[i];
    }
    return result;
}
#endif

// === Benchmark ===

template <typename F>
double bench(F&& f, int iters = 10) {
    double best = 1e18;
    for (int i = 0; i < iters; ++i) {
        auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        f();
        auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        double ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(t1 - t0).count();
        best = std::min(best, ms);
    }
    return best;
}

int main() {
    constexpr size_t N = 10'000'000;
    std::vector<float> data(N), data2(N);
    for (size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        data[i] = static_cast<float>(i % 1000) * 0.001f;
        data2[i] = static_cast<float>((i * 7) % 1000) * 0.001f;
    }

    float r;

    // Sum
    double t = bench([&]() { r = sum_scalar(data.data(), N); });
    std::cout << "Sum scalar: " << t << "ms (result=" << r << ")" << std::endl;

    #ifdef __AVX__
    t = bench([&]() { r = sum_avx(data.data(), N); });
    std::cout << "Sum AVX:    " << t << "ms (result=" << r << ")" << std::endl;
    #endif

    // Dot product
    t = bench([&]() { r = dot_scalar(data.data(), data2.data(), N); });
    std::cout << "Dot scalar: " << t << "ms (result=" << r << ")" << std::endl;

    #ifdef __AVX__
    t = bench([&]() { r = dot_avx(data.data(), data2.data(), N); });
    std::cout << "Dot AVX:    " << t << "ms (result=" << r << ")" << std::endl;
    #endif

    // Max
    t = bench([&]() { r = max_scalar(data.data(), N); });
    std::cout << "Max scalar: " << t << "ms (result=" << r << ")" << std::endl;

    #ifdef __AVX__
    t = bench([&]() { r = max_avx(data.data(), N); });
    std::cout << "Max AVX:    " << t << "ms (result=" << r << ")" << std::endl;
    #endif

    return 0;
}
```
